stem: A chemist has a 40% acid solution and pure water (0% acid). She needs to create 600 milliliters of a 15% acid solution by mixing these two liquids. How many milliliters of the 40% acid solution should she use?
explanation: Let x represent the milliliters of 40% solution. The remaining volume, (600 - x) milliliters, will be water. The amount of pure acid in the final mixture equals the amount from the 40% solution: 0.40x = 0.15(600). Simplifying: 0.40x = 90. Dividing by 0.40: x = 225. We can verify: 225 mL of 40% solution contains 0.40(225) = 90 mL of pure acid, and 90/600 = 0.15 = 15%.

distractorRationale.A: Incorrectly calculated 15% of 600 as the proportion needed, then divided by 0.40 incorrectly. = 150
distractorRationale.C: Set up the equation as 0.40x = 0.15x + 600 and solved incorrectly. = 240
distractorRationale.D: Found the amount of water needed (600 - 225) instead of the acid solution. = 375
